<content>1: ---
2: name: ai-native-repo-bootstrap
3: description: "Bootstrap, standardize, or upgrade a repository for AI-native development with clear repo context, truthful docs, modern guardrails, deterministic feedback loops, and approval-gated remote governance. Use this whenever a user wants to modernize repo docs, CI, AGENTS instructions, template residue, repo readiness for AI agents, or an audit/remediation plan for baseline files and GitHub governance, even if they do not ask for a 'bootstrap.'"
4: ---
5: 
6: # AI-Native Repo Bootstrap
7: 
8: ## Overview
9: 
10: Use this skill to bring a repository to an AI-native operating baseline so humans and AI agents can work safely, quickly, and with enough context to make good decisions.
11: 
12: This skill is not a generic template copier. It classifies repository maturity, audits local and remote state, applies or proposes the right baseline, preserves intentional maturity-based exceptions, and verifies the result.
13: 
14: Prefer judgment over box-checking. File presence alone is not enough; audit whether the repository is truthful about its current state, whether commands are actually runnable, and whether any template residue or placeholder content still misleads users or agents.
15: 
16: When GitHub governance is in scope, keep three states separate in both reasoning and reporting:
17: 
18: - local desired state stored in repo files
19: - live remote state currently configured on GitHub
20: - default-branch activation state for workflows and protections that may lag local branch changes
21: 
22: ## Use This Skill When
23: 
24: - A user wants to bootstrap a new repository for AI-native development.
25: - A repository needs stronger docs, guardrails, and feedback loops before AI agents can work effectively.
26: - A repository should be aligned with a modern baseline for `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `pre-commit`, CI, release automation, and GitHub rulesets.
27: - A docs-first repository needs an honest baseline without pretending application code or tests already exist.
28: - A repository needs an audit and remediation plan for local files plus remote GitHub governance.
29: 
30: ## Do Not Use This Skill For
31: 
32: - Product feature implementation unrelated to repository bootstrap.
33: - One-off code or CI fixes with no repository-baseline intent.
34: - Secret provisioning or credential management.
35: - Organization-wide GitHub administration outside the target repository.
36: - Destructive git cleanup or bypassing hooks.
37: 
38: ## Triage Algorithm
39: 
40: Before doing deeper work, make four decisions in this order:
41: 
42: 1. Classify the repository as `docs-first`, `early-implementation`, or `active-delivery`.
43: 2. Decide whether the template-repo lens applies because the repository itself is a reusable scaffold.
44: 3. Decide whether the request is audit-only or allows local changes. If the user asks for an audit, pressure test, or planning pass, stay audit-only and do not drift into implementation.
45: 4. Decide whether GitHub remote governance is in scope. If not, stay in the local baseline workflow and do not pull in GitHub-specific guidance.
46: 
47: These decisions keep the skill from overscoping the work, inflating maturity, or jumping into remote operations unnecessarily.
48: 
49: ## Core Workflow
50: 
51: 1. Classify repository maturity and decide early whether the template-repo lens applies.
52: 2. Audit the repository against the baseline rubric in `references/baseline-rubric.md`.
53: 3. Choose the right workflow: `new_repo` or `upgrade_repo`.
54: 4. Check for current-state truthfulness, placeholder types, and stale template residue across `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, contributor docs, and workflow comments.
55: 5. Separate minimum acceptable baseline findings from stronger target-state improvements.
56: 6. Use `references/target-state-checklist.md` when translating an audit into a concrete remediation plan so the repo gets a practical checklist, not just a scorecard.
57: 7. Apply or propose local baseline changes first.
58: 8. Report stronger target-state options not applied whenever relevant patterns are intentionally deferred.
59: 9. Audit remote GitHub governance separately using `references/github-governance.md`.
60: 10. Keep local desired state, live remote state, and default-branch activation state distinct in the report.
61: 11. Require explicit user approval before any remote `gh api` apply phase.
62: 12. Re-verify local and remote state and report residual gaps.
63: 
64: ## Workflow: `new_repo`
65: 
66: Use this workflow when the repository is empty, lightly scaffolded, or still close to raw template state.
67: 
68: ### Goals
69: 
70: - Establish the required baseline artifacts.
71: - Replace placeholders or explicitly document what remains intentionally deferred.
72: - Set up local quality loops and CI scaffolding that match current maturity.
73: - Prepare remote governance as auditable desired state.
74: 
75: ### Actions
76: 
77: 1. Confirm repo identity, purpose, and current maturity.
78: 2. Decide whether the repo is a normal docs-first repo or a template repo whose product is scaffolding itself.
79: 3. Add or update baseline repo docs and governance files.
80: 4. Add tech-agnostic local guardrails first.
81: 5. Add CI and release scaffolding that are honest about the current stage of the repo.
82: 6. If release automation is included, verify that the chosen auth path can actually push under the repo's current protection model. Do not imply a working release path when branch protection or rulesets would block release pushes.
83: 7. Prepare ruleset-as-code and remote governance guidance.
84: 8. If GitHub is in scope, document repo-settings desired state in addition to ruleset intent.
85: 9. Run validation and produce a readiness report.
86: 
87: ## Workflow: `upgrade_repo`
88: 
89: Use this workflow when the repository already has meaningful structure and needs to be brought into baseline alignment.
90: 
91: ### Goals
92: 
93: - Preserve valid repo-specific conventions.
94: - Identify missing or misaligned baseline elements.
95: - Respect intentional maturity-based exceptions.
96: - Produce an ordered remediation plan and verification report.
97: 
98: ### Actions
99: 
100: 1. Inventory the current local and remote state.
101: 2. Compare the repository to the baseline rubric.
102: 3. Classify placeholders as downstream-customization, maturity-based, stale residue, or governance-invalidating.
103: 4. Check command-to-filesystem consistency so current-state docs do not claim tooling that is not provisioned.
104: 5. Gather evidence for command realism from files such as manifests, lockfiles, scripts, workflow definitions, and tool configs, and apply the same standard to `AGENTS.md` as to `README.md`.
105: 6. Verify CODEOWNERS entries are concrete and plausibly valid for GitHub resolution, not just present.
106: 7. Treat direct remote apply examples and hook-bypass instructions as explicit safety findings when they are not fenced by audit-first or emergency-only guidance.
107: 8. If release plumbing exists or is proposed, evaluate release-path viability against the repo's actual governance posture. `GITHUB_TOKEN` is acceptable when protections permit release pushes; it becomes insufficient when protected-branch or ruleset behavior would block the release actor.
108: 9. Never invent a concrete `CODEOWNERS` user or team. Use only repo evidence or user-provided owners, and otherwise report the gap explicitly.
109: 10. Classify gaps by severity and by local versus remote scope.
110: 11. Apply or propose local fixes with minimal churn. Avoid deleting cosmetic or upstream residue unless doing so materially improves truthfulness, governance, or maintainability.
111: 12. Audit remote governance and ask for approval before any apply phase.
112: 13. Re-run validation and document residual risks.
113: 
114: ## Maturity Profiles
115: 
116: Always map the repository to a maturity profile using `references/maturity-profiles.md`.
117: 
118: - `docs-first`: planning and governance are present; implementation is absent or minimal.
119: - `early-implementation`: code exists but quality gates and automation are still forming.
120: - `active-delivery`: code, tests, release expectations, and team workflows are established.
121: 
122: Recommendations must be adjusted to the profile. Do not force active-delivery expectations onto a docs-first repository.
123: 
124: Do not let sophisticated ruleset or release automation inflate maturity by itself. A repository can remain `docs-first` even when governance and release scaffolding are advanced.
125: 
126: Template repositories need extra care. A repo whose product is reusable scaffolding may be docs-first in implementation but strong in repo operations. Evaluate whether placeholders are appropriate for downstream consumers or misleading for the current repo.
127: 
128: ## Local Baseline Expectations
129: 
130: Use `references/baseline-rubric.md` as the canonical definition of good.
131: 
132: At minimum, evaluate:
133: 
134: - repo intent and documentation clarity
135: - current-state truthfulness and command realism
136: - governance and contribution standards
137: - local feedback loops
138: - CI quality enforcement
139: - release scaffolding
140: - remote policy as code
141: - AI operability instructions
142: - environment and integration documentation
143: - stale template residue and placeholder types
144: - maturity-based exceptions
145: 
146: Treat governance-invalidating placeholders as higher severity than ordinary template text. Broken ownership, broken support links, and unsafe direct-mutation guidance should not be scored like harmless downstream customization markers.
147: 
148: Treat documentation or agent instructions that jump directly to remote mutation without an audit-first or approval-gated framing as an explicit safety smell.
149: 
150: Treat hook-bypass guidance such as `--no-verify` as a real safety finding unless it is narrowly framed as a human-only emergency exception.
151: 
152: When you need a practical implementation checklist rather than a rubric, use `references/target-state-checklist.md`.
153: 
154: ## Remote Governance Rules
155: 
156: Treat remote GitHub operations as a separate, approval-gated phase.
157: 
158: - Audit first.
159: - Show the delta clearly.
160: - Require explicit approval before create, update, or sync operations.
161: - Verify live parity after any remote apply step.
162: 
163: Use `references/github-governance.md` for repo settings, ruleset audit, ruleset sync, and parity verification patterns.
164: 
165: Treat GitHub-specific ruleset sync workflows, drift detection, and release identity integrations as stronger target-state patterns when GitHub is in scope, not as universal baseline requirements.
166: 
167: Treat release authentication as a compatibility question, not a branding preference. If the repo's protection model allows release pushes with `GITHUB_TOKEN`, that can be sufficient. If protected-branch or ruleset enforcement would block the release actor, require a bypass-capable auth path such as Octo STS or another explicitly documented alternative.
168: 
169: ## Output Standard
170: 
171: Default to `templates/delta-report-template.md` for the main report shape unless the user asks for a different format.
172: 
173: Always produce a clear report that includes:
174: 
175: 1. repository maturity classification
176: 2. compliant baseline items
177: 3. missing or misaligned items
178: 4. current-state truthfulness or command-realism issues
179: 5. stale template residue or placeholder classification findings
180: 6. minimum acceptable baseline vs stronger target-state improvements
181: 7. stronger target-state options not applied
182: 8. release path viability
183: 9. local desired state summary
184: 10. repo-settings desired-state evidence summary
185: 11. live remote state summary
186: 12. default-branch activation summary
187: 13. local remediation plan or applied changes
188: 14. remote remediation plan or applied changes
189: 15. validation results
190: 16. residual risks and follow-ups
191: 
192: Use these package resources to keep outputs consistent:
193: 
194: - `templates/delta-report-template.md`
195: - `templates/check-results-template.md`
196: - `templates/execution-log-template.json.md`
197: - `checklists/preflight-checklist.md`
198: - `checklists/verification-checklist.md`
199: - `references/target-state-checklist.md`
200: 
201: ## Safety Rules
202: 
203: - Never apply destructive operations without explicit user request.
204: - Never silently mutate remote GitHub settings or rulesets.
205: - Never claim a repository is ready without running the relevant checks.
206: - Never treat GitHub-specific guidance as universal when the neutral core is sufficient.
207: - Never invent concrete ownership metadata such as `CODEOWNERS` users or teams.
208: - Never describe release plumbing as viable unless its auth path matches the repo's protection model.
209: - Clearly label assumptions, deferred items, and unresolved questions.
210: 
211: ## Canonical Sources
212: 
213: When authoring or evolving this skill, use these examples:
214: 
215: - Generic baseline: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/base-repo-template`
216: - AI-native target-state example: `/home/damien/Liatrio/repos/forge-program-tracker`
217: 
218: The strongest guidance comes from the delta between those repositories, not from blind template reuse.
219: 
220: ## References
221: 
222: - `references/baseline-rubric.md`
223: - `references/maturity-profiles.md`
224: - `references/github-governance.md`
225: - `references/adoption-sources.md`
226: - `references/target-state-checklist.md`

(End of file - total 226 lines)
</content>

<content>1: # Maturity Profiles
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: Use these profiles to classify the target repository before recommending baseline changes.
6: 
7: The profile determines what is required now, what is recommended next, and what should be explicitly deferred.
8: 
9: Use the lowest profile that honestly fits the repository today. Advanced governance or release scaffolding should not inflate maturity if implementation is still absent.
10: 
11: ## Template-Repo Lens
12: 
13: Some repositories are themselves template products. They may be `docs-first` in implementation while still being mature in repository operations such as release automation, contribution scaffolding, or governance-as-code.
14: 
15: When auditing a template repo:
16: 
17: - separate current-repo truth from downstream customization guidance
18: - treat downstream placeholders differently from misleading current-repo placeholders
19: - do not require application-specific runtime surfaces if the template has no implementation yet
20: 
21: Examples:
22: 
23: - acceptable: a downstream `README` snippet that says "replace `{OWNER}/{REPO}` when creating your project"
24: - not acceptable: the live template repo's own `CODEOWNERS` or support links still using unresolved placeholders
25: - acceptable: a target-state architecture document clearly labeled for adopters
26: - not acceptable: present-tense setup commands for tooling the template repo does not actually provision
27: 
28: ## Profile: Docs-First
29: 
30: ### Description
31: 
32: The repository primarily contains planning, governance, architecture, or product documentation. Application code is absent or intentionally minimal.
33: 
34: ### Typical Signals
35: 
36: - strong `README.md`, `PRD`, or architecture docs
37: - little or no application code
38: - no real test suite yet
39: - CI focused on docs hygiene, policy drift, and baseline hooks
40: 
41: ### Required Baseline
42: 
43: - repo-specific docs and governance files
44: - `pre-commit` baseline
45: - CI with required status checks preserved
46: - release scaffolding with honest defaults
47: - ruleset-as-code
48: - `AGENTS.md`
49: 
50: ### Acceptable Exceptions
51: 
52: - placeholder test gate in CI
53: - empty release stamping targets
54: - commented language-specific hooks
55: - target-state architecture rather than implementation detail
56: - template scaffolding that is clearly presented as downstream customization guidance
57: 
58: ### Common Mistakes
59: 
60: - pretending tests or builds exist when they do not
61: - leaving template placeholders unresolved
62: - mixing future-state commands with current-state setup guidance
63: - deferring branch governance until later
64: - omitting AI instructions because the repo has no code yet
65: 
66: ## Profile: Early Implementation
67: 
68: ### Description
69: 
70: The repository contains real implementation work, but the delivery system and contributor workflow are still maturing.
71: 
72: ### Typical Signals
73: 
74: - application code exists
75: - at least one real lint or test command exists
76: - documentation and governance may still be partly template-derived
77: - release configuration may be scaffolded but incomplete
78: 
79: ### Required Baseline
80: 
81: - everything in `docs-first`
82: - real CI lint and test commands where available
83: - initial language-specific hooks where they add value
84: - environment examples for implemented integrations
85: 
86: ### Acceptable Exceptions
87: 
88: - partial release stamping if the packaging story is still forming
89: - limited integration documentation when systems are still being introduced
90: 
91: ### Common Mistakes
92: 
93: - keeping placeholder CI while code already exists
94: - relying on tribal knowledge for environment setup
95: - adding code without updating `AGENTS.md` or `CONTRIBUTING.md`
96: - leaving stale template comments that contradict the implemented stack
97: 
98: ## Profile: Active Delivery
99: 
100: ### Description
101: 
102: The repository is actively delivering application or platform changes with established team workflows and real automation expectations.
103: 
104: ### Typical Signals
105: 
106: - meaningful application code and tests
107: - release expectations are real, not just scaffolded
108: - dependency management and CI are part of the daily workflow
109: - reviewers depend on explicit governance and ownership
110: 
111: ### Required Baseline
112: 
113: - everything in `early-implementation`
114: - real and enforced required status checks
115: - coherent release stamping and artifacts
116: - ruleset parity or equivalent remote governance verification
117: - environment and integration docs that reflect production reality
118: 
119: ### Acceptable Exceptions
120: 
121: - only repo-specific, explicitly documented deviations justified by architecture, compliance, or team workflow
122: 
123: ### Common Mistakes
124: 
125: - release automation that exists but is not trustworthy
126: - CI drifting away from local workflows
127: - remote governance that no longer matches repo policy files
128: 
129: ## Escalation Rules
130: 
131: - If the repository looks docs-first but remote governance is missing, treat that as a real gap, not a harmless deferral.
132: - If code exists but CI still uses placeholders, escalate to `early-implementation` remediation.
133: - If release or ruleset state is live but undocumented, escalate remediation, but do not automatically reclassify the repository to `active-delivery`.
134: 
135: ## Decision Checks
136: 
137: Before finalizing the profile, ask:
138: 
139: - Are the documented commands actually runnable today?
140: - Is this repo shipping a product, or is the repo itself the product scaffold?
141: - Are advanced governance patterns real operational needs, or just prepared scaffolding?
142: - Are exceptions intentional and transparent, or are they unresolved contradictions?
143: 
144: ## Decision Rule
145: 
146: Choose the lowest profile that honestly fits the repository today, then use that profile to avoid overscoping the bootstrap work.

(End of file - total 146 lines)
</content>

<content>1: # Baseline Rubric
2: 
3: ## Purpose
4: 
5: This rubric defines what good looks like for an AI-native repository baseline.
6: 
7: Use it to audit a repository, decide what to add or upgrade, and explain why a repository is or is not ready for effective AI-assisted development.
8: 
9: Judge semantic truth, not just artifact presence. A repository with all expected files can still be misleading if commands are not runnable, comments still reflect an old template, or placeholders invalidate governance signals.
10: 
11: ## Cross-Cutting Audit Rules
12: 
13: ### Current-State Truthfulness
14: 
15: - verify that commands documented as current setup steps are supported by the repository contents
16: - separate planned stack declarations from currently runnable commands
17: - treat command-to-filesystem mismatch as a meaningful gap even when docs are otherwise strong
18: 
19: Evidence sources for command realism:
20: 
21: - package manifests such as `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, or `go.mod`
22: - lockfiles such as `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `package-lock.json`, or `uv.lock`
23: - workflow files that run the same commands in CI
24: - tool config files such as `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, `eslint.config.*`, or `tsconfig.json`
25: - scripts declared in package-manager metadata or documented bootstrap scripts
26: 
27: Audit surfaces that need the same scrutiny:
28: 
29: - `README.md`
30: - `AGENTS.md`
31: - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
32: - development/setup docs
33: - workflow comments and release notes that describe current behavior
34: 
35: Heuristics for docs-first repos:
36: 
37: - if docs use present-tense verbs like `run`, `install`, `start`, or `test`, verify supporting manifests or toolchain files exist
38: - if a planned stack is named but no manifests or scripts exist, require wording that labels commands as future-state or illustrative
39: - prefer `pre-commit` or other provisioned entrypoints over standalone CLIs that are not clearly installed by the repo
40: - treat agent-only instructions as real repo guidance, not exempt examples; `AGENTS.md` should meet the same truthfulness bar as user-facing docs
41: 
42: ### Placeholder Taxonomy
43: 
44: Classify placeholders before scoring them:
45: 
46: - `downstream-customization`: intended for adopters of a template repo
47: - `maturity-based`: acceptable because the repo has not reached that implementation stage yet
48: - `stale-template-residue`: inherited comments, framework references, or wording that no longer matches repo reality
49: - `governance-invalidating`: placeholders that weaken ownership, review, navigation, or trust in current-repo guidance
50: 
51: Not all placeholders are equal. Do not treat all placeholders as failures, and do not treat all placeholders as harmless.
52: 
53: Severity guidance:
54: 
55: - `governance-invalidating` placeholders are at least `medium` severity by default and may be `high` when they break ownership, review policy, support links, or safety boundaries
56: - `stale-template-residue` is usually `low` or `medium` depending on how misleading it is
57: - `maturity-based` and `downstream-customization` placeholders are acceptable when clearly labeled
58: 
59: Examples of governance-invalidating findings:
60: 
61: - `CODEOWNERS` entries that are placeholder-only or unlikely to resolve to real users or teams
62: - `CODEOWNERS` entries that use only an org handle such as `@org` instead of a user or `@org/team`
63: - issue-template or support links that point to broken placeholder URLs
64: - docs or agent instructions that present direct remote apply commands without audit-first or approval-gated framing
65: - docs that normalize `--no-verify` or similar hook bypasses as standard workflow guidance
66: 
67: ### Semantic Equivalence Over Exact Paths
68: 
69: Prefer meaning over exact casing or path conventions when the repo intent is clear. For example, `docs/development.md` and `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` may satisfy the same role.
70: 
71: ## Category 1: Repo Intent Is Legible
72: 
73: ### Required Signals
74: 
75: - `README.md` explains what the repo is, what state it is in, and how to get started.
76: - `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` captures implemented or target architecture.
77: - `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` provides local setup and quality commands.
78: - `AGENTS.md` gives repo-specific instructions to AI agents.
79: 
80: ### Good Looks Like
81: 
82: - minimal ambiguity about repo purpose and maturity
83: - no unresolved identity placeholders in the primary entry docs
84: - enough context for an AI agent to infer guardrails without guessing
85: - current local commands are clearly distinguishable from future-state plans
86: - stale framework or product references are absent
87: 
88: ## Category 2: Governance Is Standardized Early
89: 
90: ### Required Signals
91: 
92: - `CONTRIBUTING.md`
93: - `.github/pull_request_template.md`
94: - `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/*`
95: - `.github/CODEOWNERS`
96: 
97: ### Good Looks Like
98: 
99: - PRs explain why, what changed, and how work was validated
100: - issue templates collect actionable information
101: - ownership is concrete rather than generic placeholder-only content
102: - contribution docs match real repo gates and workflow
103: - CODEOWNERS intent is clear about whether it is advisory or enforced
104: - CODEOWNERS entries look syntactically valid for GitHub user or team resolution
105: - hook bypasses are absent from normal workflow guidance or are scoped as rare human-only exceptions
106: 
107: ## Category 3: Local Feedback Loops Exist
108: 
109: ### Required Signals
110: 
111: - `.pre-commit-config.yaml`
112: - `.markdownlint.yaml`
113: - `.gitignore`
114: 
115: ### Baseline Checks
116: 
117: - YAML validation
118: - TOML validation where relevant
119: - end-of-file fixes
120: - trailing whitespace cleanup
121: - large-file checks
122: - markdown lint and fix
123: - Conventional Commit enforcement
124: - secret scanning
125: - Renovate config validation when Renovate is present
126: 
127: ### Good Looks Like
128: 
129: - one reliable baseline verification command such as `pre-commit run --all-files`
130: - tech-agnostic checks land before language-specific hooks
131: - language-specific hooks are added when implementation maturity justifies them
132: - docs and agent instructions prefer provisioned commands over assumed globally installed tools
133: 
134: ## Category 4: CI Preserves Quality Continuously
135: 
136: ### Required Signals
137: 
138: - `.github/workflows/ci.yml`
139: 
140: ### Good Looks Like
141: 
142: - required status checks exist even in docs-first repositories
143: - CI mirrors local quality expectations rather than diverging from them
144: - maturity-aware jobs are used instead of fake implementation claims
145: - placeholder test jobs are transparent about why they exist
146: 
147: ### Docs-First Acceptable Pattern
148: 
149: - a placeholder or documentation-only test gate may exist to preserve required checks while implementation is still pending
150: 
151: ## Category 5: Release Path Is Pre-Wired
152: 
153: ### Required Signals
154: 
155: - `.github/workflows/release.yml`
156: - `.releaserc.toml`
157: 
158: ### Good Looks Like
159: 
160: - release automation is scaffolded early but configured honestly
161: - Conventional Commits are the release source of truth
162: - docs-first repositories may leave stamp targets empty if no versioned artifacts exist yet
163: - release docs do not imply artifact stamping or packaging that the repo does not actually perform yet
164: - the chosen release auth path is compatible with the repo's actual protection model
165: 
166: ### Release Viability Check
167: 
168: - treat release plumbing as nonfunctional if branch protections or rulesets would block the release actor from pushing required commits or tags
169: - `GITHUB_TOKEN` can be sufficient when the repo's protection model allows release pushes from the workflow actor
170: - require a bypass-capable auth path such as Octo STS only when protected-branch or ruleset behavior would otherwise block the chosen release pattern
171: - do not call a release path ready based only on file presence
172: 
173: ## Category 6: Remote GitHub Policy Is Treated As Code
174: 
175: ### Required Signals
176: 
177: - `.github/ruleset-config.json`
178: 
179: ### Minimum Acceptable State
180: 
181: - desired branch governance is versioned in the repo
182: - repo-level settings are at least auditable and documented if not machine-readable yet
183: - local desired state can be compared to live remote state without guessing the intended policy
184: - repo-settings desired state appears as a first-class audit output, not buried in prose examples
185: 
186: ### Stronger Signals
187: 
188: - a remote ruleset sync workflow
189: - CI drift detection against live GitHub ruleset state
190: - documented repo settings audit or apply commands
191: - repo-settings desired state captured in a table or machine-readable definition
192: 
193: ### Good Looks Like
194: 
195: - desired branch governance is versioned in the repo
196: - required checks and merge policy are explicit
197: - live GitHub state can be inspected and reconciled from the repo definition
198: - repo-level settings such as merge methods or delete-branch-on-merge are not left as pure tribal knowledge
199: - reports distinguish local desired state from live remote state and from default-branch activation status
200: 
201: ## Category 7: AI Operability Is Explicit
202: 
203: ### Required Signals
204: 
205: - `AGENTS.md`
206: 
207: ### Optional Signals
208: 
209: - `.claude/`
210: - `.cursor/`
211: - `.opencode/`
212: - other agent runtime config files that are actually used by the team
213: 
214: ### Good Looks Like
215: 
216: - repo-specific AI instructions
217: - concise quality commands
218: - explicit constraints, not generic boilerplate
219: - clear notes on placeholders, maturity, and safety expectations
220: - commands named in AI instructions are likely runnable in a fresh repo environment
221: - remote mutation guidance is clearly separated from audit steps and approval boundaries
222: 
223: ## Category 8: Environment And Integration Surfaces Are Documented
224: 
225: ### Conditional Signals
226: 
227: - `.env.example`
228: - repo docs describing required integrations
229: 
230: ### Good Looks Like
231: 
232: - placeholders only, never real secrets
233: - enough detail to understand planned external surfaces
234: - future integrations may be documented before implementation is complete
235: - variables can be annotated by phase when some are not immediately needed
236: 
237: ## Category 9: Maturity-Based Exceptions Are Intentional
238: 
239: ### Acceptable Docs-First Exceptions
240: 
241: - placeholder test job to preserve required checks
242: - empty semantic-release stamp targets
243: - commented language-specific hooks
244: - target-state architecture documentation instead of implementation-detail docs
245: - template-repo placeholders that are clearly marked as downstream customization points
246: 
247: ### Not Acceptable
248: 
249: - broken or contradictory placeholders
250: - stale template residue that points to the wrong framework, product, or workflow
251: - missing required review or status-check contracts
252: - tribal-knowledge-only remote settings
253: - generic template text that no longer matches repo reality
254: 
255: ## Minimum Acceptable State
256: 
257: A repository is minimally ready when it has:
258: 
259: - legible repo-purpose docs
260: - truthful current-state commands
261: - repo-specific governance docs
262: - runnable local baseline checks
263: - CI enforcing the baseline
264: - release scaffolding
265: - ruleset-as-code or equivalent governance intent captured in repo
266: - AI instructions in `AGENTS.md`
267: 
268: ## Strong Target State
269: 
270: A repository is strongly AI-native when it also has:
271: 
272: - remote GitHub parity audit or sync guidance
273: - ruleset drift detection
274: - maturity-aware exceptions documented clearly
275: - placeholder types explicitly separated and justified
276: - repo-specific environment and integration surfaces documented
277: - little to no gap between declared and live governance
278: - little to no stale template residue
279: 
280: For a practical implementation-oriented checklist, use `references/target-state-checklist.md`.

(End of file - total 280 lines)
</content>

# Baseline Rubric

## Purpose

This rubric defines what good looks like for an AI-native repository baseline.

Use it to audit a repository, decide what to add or upgrade, and explain why a repository is or is not ready for effective AI-assisted development.

Judge semantic truth, not just artifact presence. A repository with all expected files can still be misleading if commands are not runnable, comments still reflect an old template, or placeholders invalidate governance signals.

## Cross-Cutting Audit Rules

### Current-State Truthfulness

- verify that commands documented as current setup steps are supported by the repository contents
- separate planned stack declarations from currently runnable commands
- treat command-to-filesystem mismatch as a meaningful gap even when docs are otherwise strong

Evidence sources for command realism:

<content>1: # Target-State Checklist
2: 
3: Use this reference when converting an audit into a practical implementation or remediation plan.
4: 
5: This is not the scoring rubric. It is the operator checklist for what a good target-state repository should have, what is required now, and what can be intentionally deferred.
6: 
7: Apply the checklist in maturity order. Do not force active-delivery expectations onto a docs-first repository.
8: 
9: ## How To Use This Checklist
10: 
11: - Start with the minimum baseline for the repository's current maturity.
12: - Add stronger target-state items only when they are relevant, truthful, and supportable.
13: - When an item is relevant but intentionally deferred, report it under `Stronger Target-State Options Not Applied` rather than silently omitting it.
14: - If a GitHub-specific pattern is chosen, make sure the supporting prerequisites are also present.
15: 
16: ## Minimum Baseline Now
17: 
18: ### Repo Identity And Truthful Docs
19: 
20: - `README.md` explains what the repo is, what state it is in, and what can be run today.
21: - `CONTRIBUTING.md` reflects real contributor and validation workflow.
22: - `AGENTS.md` gives repo-specific instructions and uses only truthful commands.
23: - Development and architecture docs match current maturity and implemented stack.
24: - Present-tense setup or validation commands are backed by manifests, scripts, tool configs, or CI definitions.
25: 
26: ### Governance And Ownership
27: 
28: - `.github/pull_request_template.md` and issue templates are repo-specific.
29: - `.github/CODEOWNERS` exists when ownership is expected.
30: - `CODEOWNERS` values come from real users or teams already evidenced in the repo or supplied by the user.
31: - Broken support links, placeholder ownership, or direct mutation guidance without audit-first framing are treated as real gaps.
32: 
33: ### Local Quality Loops
34: 
35: - `.pre-commit-config.yaml` or equivalent baseline checks exist.
36: - `.markdownlint.yaml` or equivalent docs hygiene config exists when markdown is part of the repo surface.
37: - One reliable verification command exists, such as `pre-commit run --all-files`.
38: - CI mirrors the local baseline rather than describing checks the repo cannot run.
39: 
40: ### Release And Governance Baseline
41: 
42: - Release automation is either intentionally deferred or scaffolded honestly.
43: - `.releaserc.toml` and release workflow examples do not imply artifact stamping or publishing that the repo cannot actually perform.
44: - `.github/ruleset-config.json` or an equivalent governance intent file exists when GitHub rulesets are part of the repo posture.
45: - Repo-settings desired state is explicit in a file, table, or audit note when GitHub governance is in scope.
46: 
47: ## Stronger Target-State Options
48: 
49: These are valuable when relevant, but are not universal baseline requirements.
50: 
51: - Repo-settings desired state captured as a first-class artifact instead of inferred from live state.
52: - Ruleset drift detection or sync workflow.
53: - Default-branch activation checks for workflows and protections.
54: - Environment and integration docs that cover implemented or planned external systems.
55: - Maturity-aware placeholder classification documented explicitly.
56: - Strong repo-map or architecture guidance for humans and AI agents.
57: 
58: ## Release Path Viability
59: 
60: Always classify release into one of these states:
61: 
62: ### 1. Intentionally Deferred
63: 
64: - Release files may be absent, or clearly scaffolded-only.
65: - The repo does not claim live release capability yet.
66: 
67: ### 2. Present And Viable With Current Auth
68: 
69: - Release workflow and config exist.
70: - The chosen auth path can push commits, tags, or releases under the repo's actual branch-protection and ruleset model.
71: - `GITHUB_TOKEN` is acceptable when the repo's protections allow release pushes from the workflow actor.
72: 
73: ### 3. Present But Blocked By Governance/Auth Mismatch
74: 
75: - Release workflow exists, but branch protections or rulesets would block release pushes.
76: - In this case, the repo needs a bypass-capable auth path such as Octo STS or another explicitly documented alternative.
77: - Do not describe this release path as ready until the auth/protection mismatch is resolved.
78: 
79: ## GitHub-Specific Advanced Options
80: 
81: Use these only when GitHub governance is in scope and the repo's operating model justifies them.
82: 
83: ### Octo STS And Chainguard
84: 
85: - Treat Octo STS plus Chainguard identity config as one valid solution for release workflows that must push through protected-branch or ruleset enforcement.
86: - If this pattern is chosen, verify:
87:   - `octo-sts/action` appears in the release workflow
88:   - `.github/chainguard/main-semantic-release.sts.yaml` or equivalent identity file exists
89:   - the release identity and ruleset bypass story are documented
90: - Do not require Octo STS when `GITHUB_TOKEN` is already viable under the repo's protection model.
91: 
92: ### Ruleset Drift Detection
93: 
94: - Remote parity checks or ruleset-sync workflows are stronger target-state patterns for repos already treating GitHub policy as code.
95: 
96: ### Repo Settings As Code Or Audit Table
97: 
98: - Capture merge policy, delete-branch-on-merge, issues/wiki posture, and similar settings explicitly when they matter to repo operations.
99: 
100: ## Things To Avoid
101: 
102: - Inventing `CODEOWNERS` users or teams.
103: - Deleting cosmetic or upstream residue unless doing so materially improves truthfulness, governance, or maintainability.
104: - Marking advanced GitHub integrations as universal requirements.
105: - Marking release automation as ready when the auth path is blocked by governance.
106: - Presenting raw `gh api` apply commands as the default first step.
107: 
108: ## Verification Before Calling The Repo Ready
109: 
110: - Current-state docs are truthful.
111: - The minimum baseline for the repo's maturity is satisfied.
112: - Stronger target-state options not applied are reported explicitly.
113: - Release-path viability is classified honestly.
114: - Ownership and governance metadata are concrete or explicitly unresolved.
115: - Remote apply steps, if any, remain approval-gated.

(End of file - total 115 lines)
</content>

+# Delta Report
+
+## Repository Summary
+
+- Repository path: not provided in the eval prompt; this is a scenario-based bootstrap plan for an existing early-implementation repository.
+- Repository purpose: existing software repository with real code, weak governance, missing AI-operability docs, and no proven release prerequisites.
+- Current maturity profile: `early-implementation`.
+- Template-repo lens needed: no.
+- Workflow used: `upgrade_repo`.
+
+## State Separation
+
+- Local desired state: truthful repo docs, repo-specific contributor guidance, `AGENTS.md`, concrete or explicitly unresolved ownership metadata, baseline local checks, and CI that runs only real supported commands.
+- Repo-settings desired-state evidence: not yet captured; add an audit note or versioned artifact that states the intended merge policy, required checks, and branch-governance expectations.
+- Live remote state: unknown from the prompt; weak governance is assumed, but no remote audit evidence is available yet.
+- Default-branch activation state: unknown until workflows, protections, and required checks are audited on the default branch.
+
+## Compliant Baseline Items
+
+- Real implementation exists, so the repository should be treated as `early-implementation` rather than `docs-first`.
+- The repo already has enough substance to justify replacing fake or placeholder process guidance with truthful, minimum-baseline controls.
+- The repo should preserve any existing working commands and conventions that are already real and useful.
+
+## Truthfulness And Command Realism
+
+- Current runnable commands: not evidenced in the prompt; first remediation step is to inventory real setup, lint, test, and build commands from manifests, scripts, tool configs, and CI.
+- Future-state commands that are clearly labeled: stronger governance sync, release automation, and advanced repo settings can be documented as deferred target state only.
+- Contradictions or unsupported commands: treat any stale template docs, placeholder CI jobs, or agent instructions that describe non-existent commands as priority cleanup because the repo already contains real code.
+
+## Placeholder And Template Residue Findings
+
+- Downstream-customization placeholders: none assumed; this does not read like a template repository.
+- Maturity-based placeholders: release automation may remain deferred if the repo does not yet have a proven packaging and auth story.
+- Stale template residue: likely in docs, contributor guidance, or CI comments if the repository still reflects scaffold-era wording instead of the implemented stack.
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders: `CODEOWNERS` entries must stay unresolved unless real owners are evidenced; placeholder-only owners, broken support links, or direct remote-apply guidance would be medium-to-high severity findings.
+
+## Gaps By Severity
+
+### High
+
+- Missing `AGENTS.md` or equivalent AI-operability guidance for a repo that already has real code.
+- Missing or placeholder governance metadata if ownership, review routing, or support escalation cannot be trusted.
+- Any docs or CI claims that imply release readiness without a proven auth path, branch-protection fit, and versioning target.
+
+### Medium
+
+- Stale template docs in `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, PR templates, or workflow comments that no longer match the implemented stack.
+- Placeholder CI that does not execute the repo's real lint or test entrypoints.
+- Repo-settings intent and branch-governance expectations living only as tribal knowledge instead of an auditable desired-state note.
+
+### Low
+
+- Cosmetic wording cleanup that does not improve truthfulness, governance, or maintainability.
+- Opportunistic restructuring of docs solely for style consistency.
+
+## Minimum Baseline Vs Stronger Target State
+
+- Minimum acceptable baseline gaps: add truthful `README.md` state language, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and `AGENTS.md`; make PR and issue templates repo-specific; add or tighten `pre-commit` baseline checks; ensure CI runs only real supported commands; document release as deferred until prerequisites are proven; capture governance desired state in repo; leave `CODEOWNERS` explicitly unresolved if owners are unknown.
+- Stronger target-state improvements: ruleset drift detection, remote sync workflows, repo-settings-as-code, richer architecture and repo-map docs, integration surface documentation, and release automation with a verified auth strategy.
+
+## Stronger Target-State Options Not Applied
+
+- Relevant but intentionally deferred: automated ruleset sync, default-branch activation checks, advanced release automation, and expanded environment or integration docs beyond what the current implementation already needs.
+- Blocked by missing org or repo prerequisites: concrete `CODEOWNERS` owners, required-check enforcement details, and release auth design all depend on verified team ownership and remote governance posture.
+
+## Release Path Viability
+
+- Current release state: `intentionally deferred` until the repo proves what is versioned, how releases should be stamped, and whether the workflow actor can operate under branch protections or rulesets.
+- Chosen auth path: not selected yet; do not assume `GITHUB_TOKEN`, Octo STS, or another path until governance is audited.
+- Protection-model compatibility notes: no release path should be described as ready before default-branch protections, rulesets, and push requirements are checked against the intended release actor.
+- Required prerequisites not yet present: explicit versioning target, honest release workflow scope, branch-protection audit, and a documented auth strategy that matches the protection model.
+
+## Governance Placeholder Severity
+
+- Governance-invalidating placeholders: unresolved `CODEOWNERS` handles, placeholder support contacts, or raw remote-mutation instructions presented as standard workflow guidance.
+- Advisory placeholders that are acceptable for now: clearly labeled future-state notes for advanced governance or release automation.
+- CODEOWNERS validity or enforcement notes: create `.github/CODEOWNERS` only with real users or teams already evidenced in the repo or supplied by the user; otherwise leave the file absent or explicitly marked as unresolved in the plan.
+- Unsafe direct-apply or hook-bypass guidance: keep remote governance audit-only and do not normalize `--no-verify` or direct `gh api` mutation as default bootstrap steps.
+
+## Local Remediation Plan
+
+- Planned changes: 1) audit actual commands and implemented stack; 2) update `README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and development docs to describe only what runs today; 3) add repo-specific `AGENTS.md`; 4) replace placeholder CI with real lint and test entrypoints where they already exist; 5) add a minimal `pre-commit` baseline and markdown hygiene; 6) make PR and issue templates repo-specific; 7) document release as deferred or blocked rather than ready; 8) record ownership and governance gaps explicitly instead of guessing.
+- Applied changes: none; planning-only eval.
+- Deferred local items: architecture deep-dives, large doc rewrites, repo-map expansion, and cleanup of cosmetic residue that does not improve trustworthiness.
+
+## Remote Governance Plan
+
+- Audited areas: none yet; remote state was not inspected for this planning-only scenario.
+- Proposed remote changes: audit current branch protections, required checks, merge policy, and repository settings; compare them to a versioned desired-state note; then propose the minimum remote changes needed to support the local baseline.
+- Approval required before apply: yes.
+- Applied remote changes: none.
+- Deferred remote items: live ruleset sync, drift detection, and release-actor bypass design until audit evidence exists and approval is given.
+
+## Validation Results
+
+- Commands run: none against a target repository; this output is a scenario-based remediation plan derived from the skill guidance.
+- Passing checks: the plan keeps the repo classified as `early-implementation`, prioritizes the minimum honest baseline first, leaves unknown ownership unresolved, and reports stronger target-state options as intentionally deferred.
+- Failing or skipped checks: repository-specific command audit, remote-governance audit, release-path verification, and CODEOWNERS validation are all still pending actual repo inspection.
+
+## Residual Risks And Follow-Ups
+
+- If stale docs or CI currently overclaim what the repo can do, trust will remain low until truthfulness is fixed before any broader cleanup.
+- If ownership stays undefined, governance improvements will remain advisory and review routing may still be unreliable.
+- If release scaffolding is added before auth and protection compatibility are proven, the repo may appear more ready than it actually is.
+- Next execution should start with a file-backed inventory of real commands, then land only the smallest set of doc, governance, and CI changes needed to make the repository honest and operable.
